system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
You are presented with 5 anonymous responses to the same brief. They are labeled E1 through E5.

**E1:** The most appropriate policy for our AI health coaching feature is **Option A: local-only processing**. Health data is the most intimate and regulated personal information we handle, and with 2 million monthly active users, our exposure to reputational, legal, and compliance risk from a single breach or misuse is enormous. Processing all inference locally on the device removes that risk structurally: no health data ever leaves the phone.

**E2:** I recommend **Option B: encrypted cloud processing with no training on user health data** as the primary policy. For a consumer health app at this scale, the default should optimize for **trust, regulatory defensibility, and clinical-quality capability**. Health data is among the most sensitive categories of personal information, and an AI coaching feature will likely involve intimate patterns: symptoms, medications, reproductive health, mental health, fitness, sleep, and behavioral data.

**E3:** # Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted Cloud with No Training

For a direct-to-consumer health app at 2M MAU launching an AI coaching feature, **Option B is the right primary policy**. It is the only choice that delivers full capability without compromising the trust foundation that health apps live and die on. **Why B wins on the axes that matter most:**

Health data is uniquely sensitive — it touches identity, insurability, family, and mental health.

**E4:** I recommend **Option D: Cloud with opt-in training only**. For a direct-to-consumer health app collecting sensitive data at scale, the governing policy must simultaneously enable effective care, maintain regulatory defensibility, and earn durable user trust. Option D is the only path that meets all three imperatives without unacceptable tradeoffs.

**E5:** I strongly recommend **Option B: Encrypted cloud with no training**. For a direct-to-consumer health app operating at a scale of 2 million monthly active users, trust is not just a brand pillar—it is your primary competitive moat. When introducing an AI health coach, users will only engage meaningfully if they feel completely safe sharing their most intimate physical and behavioral data.

Your task: for each of the following triads of 3 responses, identify a single bipolar construct that distinguishes them. Specifically, identify how TWO of the three are similar to each other in a way that distinguishes them from the THIRD. Name the two poles of that dimension.

Triads to analyze:
Triad 1: E1, E2, E4
Triad 2: E2, E3, E4
Triad 3: E1, E2, E3

For each triad, output EXACTLY this format with no markdown, no bold, no bullets, no numbered lists:
Triad 1:
Left pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>
Right pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>

Triad 2:
Left pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>
Right pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>

Triad 3:
Left pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>
Right pole: <short phrase, max 12 words>

Be specific. Avoid generic dichotomies like 'good vs bad'. Use plain text labels (Triad N, Left pole, Right pole) without any markdown formatting.
raw_response_content:
Triad 1:
Left pole: Cloud-based processing for richer capability
Right pole: Local-only processing for maximum containment

Triad 2:
Left pole: No training on user health data
Right pole: Opt-in training with user consent

Triad 3:
Left pole: Encrypted cloud inference without training
Right pole: On-device inference only
